title: Object-Oriented Programming
description: Hand-written closed questions reviewed file by file from the OOP Markdown lecture sources.

questions.prompt: What is the course name listed in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: The syllabus names the course Advanced Object Oriented Programming.

questions.prompt: Which textbook is listed for the OOP course?
choices: Object-Oriented Programming in C++ by Robert Lafore
questions.explanation: The syllabus lists Object-Oriented Programming in C++ by Robert Lafore as a textbook.

questions.prompt: Which pair appears as an early syllabus comparison?
choices: Structural/Procedural Programming vs Object Oriented Programming
questions.explanation: The first syllabus topic compares structural/procedural programming with OOP.

questions.prompt: Which topic is grouped with encapsulation in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: The syllabus writes encapsulation together with data hiding.

questions.prompt: Which class-related topic is listed after access levels?
questions.explanation: The syllabus lists the scope resolution operator after class/object/access-level topics.

questions.prompt: Which polymorphism topic is explicitly named in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: The syllabus includes polymorphism through operator overloading, including unary and binary overloading.

questions.prompt: Which inheritance detail is included in the syllabus?
questions.explanation: The inheritance section lists terms, types, modes, single inheritance, and multiple inheritance.

questions.prompt: Which later syllabus topic follows virtual base classes?
questions.explanation: The syllabus lists Template and Standard Template Library after virtual base classes.

questions.prompt: In the pointer example, after `p = a`, what does `*p` print first?
questions.explanation: Assigning the array name to the pointer makes the pointer refer to the first element.

questions.prompt: What operation is used in the notes to move the pointer to the next array element?
questions.explanation: The hint uses `p++` before printing the second value.

questions.prompt: Which two function categories are reviewed in lecture 1?
choices: Standard library functions and user-defined functions
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly contrasts standard library functions with user-defined functions.

questions.prompt: Why does the `display(num, nam, addr)` function receive parameters?
choices: To print saved student details passed from `main`
To allocate dynamic memory for an array
questions.explanation: The display function receives the number, name, and address then prints them.

questions.prompt: What is a function called when it calls itself?
questions.explanation: The notes ask whether a function can call itself and name that a recursive function.

questions.prompt: How does the `po(int *p)` example receive an array?
choices: Through a pointer to its first element
Through a copy of the whole array object
questions.explanation: The array is passed as `po(a)`, where `a` decays to a pointer to the first element.

questions.prompt: What does `cout << *p++` do inside the array-printing loop?
choices: Prints the current pointed value, then advances the pointer
questions.explanation: The example prints each pointed value and increments the pointer through the array.

questions.prompt: When an array of `struct st` is passed to `po(struct st *s2)`, what can the function do?
choices: Modify the original student records through the pointer
questions.explanation: The function writes new values through `s2[i]`, so the original array in `main` is changed.

questions.prompt: What is a programming paradigm?
choices: A style for organizing and thinking about programs
questions.explanation: The lecture defines paradigms as styles or ways of organizing programs.

questions.prompt: According to the lecture, what is a programming paradigm NOT?
choices: A language or tool by itself
A set of ideas and guidelines
A way to structure a program
questions.explanation: The notes say paradigms are not languages or tools and cannot build anything alone.

questions.prompt: Why are there many paradigms?
choices: Different problems and projects need different structures
Every program must use only one fixed roadmap
All languages have exactly the same features
questions.explanation: The lecture compares small apps with safety-critical systems to show different needs.

questions.prompt: What does imperative programming emphasize?
choices: Detailed step-by-step instructions describing how to do the task
questions.explanation: Imperative code tells the computer exactly what to execute and in what order.

questions.prompt: What feature does procedural programming add to imperative programming?
choices: Functions or procedures to improve modularity
questions.explanation: Procedural programming subdivides execution into functions, procedures, or subroutines.

questions.prompt: What is a pure function in the functional programming section?
choices: A function that relies only on inputs and produces no side effects
A function that always prints to the screen
A function that changes global data every time
A function that can only be used inside a class
questions.explanation: The lecture says pure functions use only inputs and do not change outside state.

questions.prompt: How does declarative programming differ from imperative programming?
choices: It describes what result is needed while hiding many execution details
It requires every loop to be written manually
It requires all data to be public
questions.explanation: The lecture uses filter/map/reduce-style examples to show saying what is wanted, not coding every step.

questions.prompt: What is the core idea of object orientation in this lecture?
choices: Separating concerns into objects with properties and methods
Putting all data and all logic in one global procedure
questions.explanation: The lecture says object orientation separates concerns into entities coded as objects.

questions.prompt: What are objects created from a class called?
questions.explanation: The lecture states that objects created from a class are called instances.

questions.prompt: Which concept does the lecture connect with packing data and functionality together?
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that an object or class encapsulates data and functionality.

questions.prompt: In the class syntax shown, where are member functions usually exposed?
questions.explanation: The general syntax lists private attributes and public member functions.

questions.prompt: Why does the student example use `setdata` before storing values?
choices: To allow validation or filtering before member data is changed
To convert the class into a struct
questions.explanation: The lecture says passing data to a member function first lets you filter it before use.

questions.prompt: What does the elevator constructor do in the first constructor example?
choices: Initializes the floor to zero automatically
questions.explanation: The constructor sets `flr = 0` so the elevator starts at floor zero.

questions.prompt: Which operator is used in `void elevator::display()`?
questions.explanation: `::` defines the member function outside the class body.

questions.prompt: What is constructor overloading?
choices: Defining constructors with different parameter lists
questions.explanation: The lecture shows both `elevator()` and `elevator(int fl)`.

questions.prompt: What does `~elevator()` represent?
questions.explanation: A tilde before the class name declares the destructor.

questions.prompt: When is a constructor called?
choices: When an object of the class is created
Only when the object is printed
questions.explanation: The elevator examples rely on the constructor running when `elevator elv` is created.

questions.prompt: Which homework topic is tied to copying objects?
questions.explanation: The lecture asks what the default copy constructor is.

questions.prompt: What does `Counter() : count(0)` do?
choices: Initializes `count` to zero using an initializer list
questions.explanation: The constructor initializer list sets the counter value to zero.

questions.prompt: What is true about object data and member functions according to the memory section?
choices: Each object has its own data, but objects of the same class use the same member functions
Every object has a separate copy of every function in memory
Static data is copied into every object
questions.explanation: The lecture corrects the first impression by saying member functions are shared by objects of a class.

questions.prompt: What does static class data provide?
choices: One shared data item for the whole class
One separate copy for every function call
questions.explanation: Static class member data is created once for the class and shared among its objects.

questions.prompt: Why does the static member example include `int increment::count = 0;` outside the class?
choices: To define the static data member
questions.explanation: The class contains the declaration, while the outside line provides the definition.

questions.prompt: What is demonstrated by `calculateAverage(Student s1, Student s2)`?
questions.explanation: The function takes Student objects as parameters and reads their marks.

questions.prompt: In the Distance example, why does `add_dist` check whether inches are at least 12.0?
choices: To carry extra inches into feet
To reset all distances to zero
questions.explanation: When inches exceed 12, the example subtracts 12 inches and increments feet.

questions.prompt: What does returning objects from functions allow in the Distance section?
choices: Assigning the result of object operations, such as `dist3 = dist1.add_dist(dist2)`
questions.explanation: The lecture moves from a void-style add function to assigning a returned Distance object.

questions.prompt: Function overloading means functions share a name but differ by what?
choices: Type, number, or order of parameters
questions.explanation: The notes list type, number, and ordering as the differences.

questions.prompt: What is constructor overloading in the elevator example?
choices: Providing both a no-argument constructor and a constructor with an initial floor
questions.explanation: The elevator class has `elevator()` and `elevator(int fl)`.

questions.prompt: What constructor is created if you define no constructor in a class?
questions.explanation: The note says a no-argument default constructor is created when no constructor is defined.

questions.prompt: What is an operator?
choices: A symbol used in an operation, such as arithmetic or logical work
questions.explanation: The lecture defines operators as symbols used in arithmetic, logical, relational, and other operations.

questions.prompt: Why overload an operator for objects?
choices: To tell the compiler what that operator means for a user-defined type
To make every data member public
To remove the need for classes
questions.explanation: The lecture explains that the compiler needs a special member function to understand operators on objects.

questions.prompt: Which operators are unary operators?
choices: Operators that act on one operand, such as `++` and `--`
Operators that must act on two classes
questions.explanation: Unary operators act on one operand; the lecture uses increment and decrement as examples.

questions.prompt: What declaration overloads prefix increment for `Counter` in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows `void operator ++ ()` as the member function called for `++c1`.

questions.prompt: How does the compiler decide between built-in `++x` and user-written `operator++()`?
choices: By the comment before the statement
questions.explanation: If the operand is an integer the built-in version is used; if it is a Counter object the overloaded operator is used.

questions.prompt: Why can't `c1 = ++c2` work when `operator++()` returns `void`?
choices: The assignment needs a returned Counter object
The prefix form can never be overloaded
Objects cannot be assigned in C++
questions.explanation: The expression needs the result of `++c2` to be assigned to `c1`; `void` provides no value.

questions.prompt: Which return approach creates a named object, copies the count into it, then returns it?
questions.explanation: The lecture's temporary-object method creates `Counter temp`, sets `temp.count`, and returns it.

questions.prompt: Why does the nameless temporary object example need `Counter(int c)`?
choices: Because `return Counter(count)` constructs an unnamed Counter with one argument
Because all constructors must have exactly one argument
questions.explanation: The returned unnamed object is built with the current count, so a one-argument constructor is required.

questions.prompt: Which benefit of operator overloading is listed in the lecture?
choices: Forcing all data to be global
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists readability and extendibility as support from operator overloading.

questions.prompt: In the pharmacy review example, what does `++Panadol` represent conceptually?
choices: Selling one item and increasing its item count
questions.explanation: The lecture describes a barcode-reader situation where selling one item increments its count.

questions.prompt: What does the temporary-object version of unary `++` return?
choices: An Items object containing the updated count
A raw pointer to the class name
questions.explanation: The function increments `item_count`, stores it in a temporary Items object, and returns that object.

questions.prompt: Why does the nameless object example include `Items(int c)`?
questions.explanation: The unnamed return object is created with one argument, so the constructor must accept one argument.

questions.prompt: What are binary operators?
choices: Operators that act on two operands
Operators that act on one operand only
questions.explanation: The lecture defines binary operators as operators acting on two operands, such as `+`, `-`, and `*`.

questions.prompt: In `Sedative = Panadol + Aspirin`, which object is the left operand and member-object for `operator+`?
questions.explanation: For a member operator, the object on the left side is the object whose member function is called.

questions.prompt: In the same `operator+` example, how is the right operand supplied?
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that the right-side object, Aspirin, is passed as the argument to the operator function.

questions.prompt: What does the binary `operator+` return in the Items example?
choices: An Items object whose count is the sum of the two counts
A pointer to the first item only
A string containing the class name
Nothing, because binary operators must be void
questions.explanation: The example adds the item counts and returns an Items object with the total.

questions.prompt: Why is `Sedative = 15 + Panadol` a problem for the shown member `operator+`?
choices: The left operand is an integer, so there is no Items member operator to call
The right operand must always be an integer
questions.explanation: A member operator is selected from the left operand; an integer does not have the Items `operator+` member.

questions.prompt: How does lecture 7 summarize encapsulation?
choices: Packing data and functions into a single component
Calling a base pointer at runtime
questions.explanation: The lecture reviews encapsulation as data hiding and packing data with functions.

questions.prompt: How does lecture 7 summarize inheritance?
choices: Acquiring properties of a parent class in a subclass object
Giving one function many parameter lists
questions.explanation: The lecture defines inheritance as acquiring properties from parent class to subclass object.

questions.prompt: Which major benefit of inheritance is emphasized first?
questions.explanation: The lecture says inheritance supports reusability instead of recreating the same classes repeatedly.

questions.prompt: Which list matches benefits of inheritance from the lecture?
choices: Shared code, lower complexity, flexibility, extendibility, overriding
Packet routing, encryption, scheduling, paging, caching
Only slower code and more duplication
Only private access and no reuse
questions.explanation: The lecture lists shared code, reducing complexity, flexibility, extendibility, and overriding.

questions.prompt: What is a subclass?
choices: The class that inherits properties from another class
The class whose properties are inherited
questions.explanation: The notes identify subclass and derived class as the inheriting class.

questions.prompt: What is a superclass?
choices: The base class whose properties are inherited
questions.explanation: The superclass is also called the base class.

questions.prompt: Which inheritance type is described as a child inheriting through father and grandfather?
questions.explanation: The lecture explains multilevel inheritance with father and grandfather.

questions.prompt: What is hybrid inheritance?
choices: A combination of two or more inheritance types
Inheritance from exactly one class only
questions.explanation: The lecture says hybrid inheritance combines two or more types and may be called multipath inheritance.

questions.prompt: Which syntax form matches single inheritance from the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows `class derived_class_name : access_specifier base_class_name`.

questions.prompt: In public inheritance, what happens to public base members?
choices: They remain public in the derived class
questions.explanation: The public mode keeps public base members public in the derived class.

questions.prompt: In protected inheritance, what happens to public and protected base members?
choices: Both become protected in the derived class
Both become public in the derived class
questions.explanation: The lecture says protected mode makes both public and protected base members protected in the derived class.

questions.prompt: In private inheritance, what happens to public and protected base members?
choices: Both become private in the derived class
questions.explanation: The private mode makes inherited public and protected base members private in the derived class.

questions.prompt: Why must the base class be written before the derived class in the example?
choices: The compiler must know the base class before deriving from it
Derived classes can never use public data
It is required only for comments
questions.explanation: The lecture notes the Base class must be written before Derived.

questions.prompt: In the example where `Base` has private `property` and public `show`, how can `Derived` use it?
choices: By calling the inherited public `show` member
questions.explanation: The derived object calls `d.show(19)` even though the actual data member is private inside Base.

questions.prompt: Which syntax is shown for multiple inheritance?
choices: class Graduate: public Person, public student
questions.explanation: The lecture shows multiple base classes separated by a comma.

questions.prompt: What does the Bat example demonstrate?
choices: A class deriving from both Mammal and Winged_Animal
A template class with two type parameters
A friend function between two classes
questions.explanation: The class `Bat` inherits from both `Mammal` and `Winged_Animal`.

questions.prompt: What does polymorphism mean in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture begins by defining polymorphism as many forms.

questions.prompt: What is a virtual function?
choices: A base-class member function that can be overridden by a derived class and called dynamically
A variable that stores an address
A function that cannot be inherited
questions.explanation: The lecture says a virtual function is declared in a base class and redefined by a derived class.

questions.prompt: What do virtual functions mainly achieve?
questions.explanation: The lecture says virtual functions are mainly used to achieve runtime polymorphism.

questions.prompt: How should virtual functions be accessed to achieve runtime polymorphism?
choices: Using a pointer or reference of base class type
questions.explanation: The rules say virtual functions should be accessed using base-class pointers or references.

questions.prompt: Which rule about virtual functions is stated in the lecture?
choices: Virtual functions must always be private
questions.explanation: The lecture lists that virtual functions cannot be static.

questions.prompt: In the shape example, why is `ptrarr[j]->draw()` powerful?
choices: The same call can execute different draw functions depending on the actual object
It forces every shape to be a triangle
It copies every object into the array
questions.explanation: A ball pointer calls the ball draw function, while a triangle pointer calls the triangle draw function.

questions.prompt: Without `virtual`, what does `Base* b = &d; b->print();` call in the lecture example?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows the output is the Base class message when `print` is not virtual.

questions.prompt: What problem do virtual base classes solve?
choices: Duplicate base-class subobjects in multiple-inheritance hierarchies
questions.explanation: Virtual base classes prevent multiple instances of the same base from appearing through multiple inheritance paths.

questions.prompt: Why is `basedata` ambiguous in the non-virtual diamond example?
choices: Grandchild inherits two separate Parent subobjects through Child1 and Child2
The variable is declared as public
questions.explanation: Each intermediate child has its own Parent copy, so Grandchild has two possible `basedata` members.

questions.prompt: What does `class Child1 : virtual public Parent` cause in the virtual-base example?
choices: Child1 and Child2 share one common Parent subobject in Grandchild
questions.explanation: The `virtual` keyword makes the two paths share a single Parent copy, removing ambiguity.

questions.prompt: What is a friend function?
choices: A non-member function granted access to private and protected class data
A virtual function that must be static
A function that belongs to every class automatically
questions.explanation: The lecture says a friend function does not belong to the class but can access its data members.

questions.prompt: Where is the `friend` keyword placed?
choices: In the function declaration inside the class
questions.explanation: The lecture says `friend` is placed in the declaration, not in the function definition.

questions.prompt: How is the friend `sum(point)` function called in the example?
choices: As `sum(p1)` without object name and dot operator
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that friend functions are called without an object name or dot operator.

questions.prompt: In which class section can a friend function be declared?
questions.explanation: The notes say a friend function can be declared in any section of the class.

questions.prompt: Why are friend functions described as bridges?
choices: They can operate on private data from two different classes
They force all classes to inherit from one base
questions.explanation: The bridge example adds private members from pointA and pointB.

questions.prompt: Why is `class pointB;` used before defining `pointA`?
choices: It forward-declares pointB so the friend function can mention it
questions.explanation: The two-class friend example needs pointB known before pointA declares `friend int add(pointA, pointB)`.

questions.prompt: Which advantage of friend functions is listed?
choices: They can access members without inheriting the class
They remove the need for objects
questions.explanation: The advantages section says friend functions access members without needing inheritance.

questions.prompt: Which disadvantage of friend functions is listed?
choices: They can violate data hiding by accessing private members from outside
They cannot read any private data
They force every member to be static
questions.explanation: The lecture warns that friend functions violate the law of data hiding.

questions.prompt: What happens when a class is declared as a friend class?
choices: All member functions of the friend class become friend functions
It becomes the base class automatically
It loses access to all data
questions.explanation: The friend-class section states that all member functions of the friend class become friend functions.

questions.prompt: Is friendship in C++ automatically mutual?
choices: No, friendship is granted, not taken
Yes, both classes always access each other
No, friend classes cannot access private data
questions.explanation: The lecture says ClassB can access ClassA if granted friendship, but ClassA cannot automatically access ClassB.

questions.prompt: Why is function overloading introduced before templates?
choices: To show the problem of writing similar functions for different data types
To show that templates cannot use functions
questions.explanation: The lecture asks what happens when doubles are passed to an int sum function, then shows overloading as one solution.

questions.prompt: What do templates provide in C++?
choices: Code that is independent of a specific data type
A way to delete objects automatically
questions.explanation: The lecture defines templates as generic code that works on any data type passed as a parameter.

questions.prompt: What is another name for the template data-type parameter?
questions.explanation: The notes say the parameter that passes the data type is also called a type name.

questions.prompt: What are the two template forms listed?
questions.explanation: The lecture says templates can be implemented as function templates or class templates.

questions.prompt: Which syntax matches the function template example?
choices: template <class T> T sum(T a1, T b1)
questions.explanation: The lecture shows a generic sum function with `template <class T>`.

questions.prompt: Why use multiple template parameters such as `<class T1, class T2>`?
choices: To support arguments of different data types
To force all arguments to be integers
questions.explanation: The lecture uses an int and a double to motivate separate template parameters.

questions.prompt: What does `func_swap(T &arg1, T &arg2)` demonstrate?
choices: A function template using references to swap values of several types
A friend class accessing private data
questions.explanation: The example swaps ints, doubles, and chars using one template function.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a class template?
choices: To define a class whose member data/functions can work with different data types
To make all members private forever
To remove the need for objects
To prevent constructors from receiving arguments
questions.explanation: The lecture explains that template classes avoid writing separate classes for each data type.

questions.prompt: Which object declaration matches the template class syntax?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows objects such as `className<int>`, `className<float>`, and `className<char>`.

questions.prompt: How does the lecture compare `typename` and `class` in template declarations?
choices: They are semantically equivalent in common template declarations
questions.explanation: The lecture says `typename` and `class` are semantically equivalent for declaring template type parameters, with special cases noted.

exams.title: OOP Projects
exams.description: Manual questions from the projects Markdown source.

questions.prompt: What validation is required in the Hard Currency Converter project?
choices: Validate that entered money values are numeric
Validate that all input is an image
Validate that no exit option exists
Validate that every user is an administrator
questions.explanation: The project description requires checking the entered values so conversion works only on numeric data.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the ATM Card project?
choices: Verify card validity before allowing simple financial operations
questions.explanation: The ATM project centers on validating card data before withdrawal, transfer, balance checking, and similar work.

questions.prompt: Which two calculator modes are requested?
questions.explanation: The calculator project asks for simple and scientific calculator options.

questions.prompt: Which operations are required in the Student Management System?
choices: Add, delete, edit, and display student data
questions.explanation: The student-management project describes adding, deleting, editing, and displaying student records.

questions.prompt: What storage restriction is mentioned for the Student Management System?
choices: Do not save the data permanently in a file
Store every record only in a PDF
questions.explanation: The description says data should be modified during execution and not stored permanently in a file.

questions.prompt: Which action in the Bookshop Management System should request confirmation?
questions.explanation: The bookshop project requires confirmation before deleting a book record.

questions.prompt: Which operations are named for the Bank Account Management System?
choices: Deposit, withdrawal, display, search, edit, and delete account data
questions.explanation: The bank account project includes creating accounts plus deposit, withdrawal, display, search, edit, and delete features.

questions.prompt: What should the Super Market Billing System display after purchases?
choices: Purchased items, their values/details, and the total
questions.explanation: The supermarket billing project requires showing all purchased items and the total amount.

questions.prompt: Which features are required by the Phone Directory project?
choices: Add, display, edit, search, and delete phone data
questions.explanation: The phone directory project lists adding, displaying, editing, searching, and deleting phone records.

questions.prompt: What information is suggested for a bus reservation ticket?
choices: Bus number, bus brand, seat number, booked/free seats, and ticket price
Only a pointer to one integer
questions.explanation: The bus reservation project lists bus number, brand, seat information, and ticket price among ticket details.
